rubric.description: Reframes vague asks into crisp problem statements; clarifies who the user is, what success looks like, and what's in or out of scope.
Reasons about effort, impact, and risk; can defend why something is NOT being built; uses frameworks without getting captured by them.
Aligns engineering, design, and leadership; surfaces disagreements early; writes and speaks with the right level of detail for the audience.
Defines outcome metrics rather than vanity numbers; understands lagging vs. leading, guardrails, and how to read an A/B test honestly.
Drives clarity day-to-day; unblocks teams; ships in slices; knows when to cut scope vs. extend timeline; closes the loop after launch.

items.prompt: Tell me about a product or feature you've shipped that you're proud of. What was the problem, your hypothesis, and what shipped?
followUpHints: Push for what THEY drove vs. the team
Ask how they validated the problem before building
Probe for what the metrics actually showed after launch

items.prompt: Your CEO walks up and says 'engagement is down, figure out what to do.' Walk me through how you'd respond in the next 48 hours.
followUpHints: Probe for clarifying questions BEFORE jumping to solutions
Ask how they'd segment the data
Push on the difference between 'engagement' and an actual user outcome

items.prompt: You have three feature requests: a top customer threatening to churn wants A; sales wants B for a deal; engineering says C is critical tech debt. How do you decide?
followUpHints: Probe for what data they'd gather before deciding
Ask what they'd say no to, and how they'd say it
Push on whether the loudest voice is the right voice

items.prompt: You're launching a new onboarding flow. What metric(s) would you watch in week one, week four, and quarter one? Why those?
followUpHints: Probe for activation vs. retention vs. revenue distinction
Ask about guardrail metrics, not just primary
Push on what would make them roll the change back

items.prompt: An A/B test shows your variant is up 2% on the primary metric, but engineering thinks the experiment was buggy. How do you handle it?
followUpHints: Probe for whether they'd kill the test, re-run, or ship
Ask what 'buggy' specifically means and how they'd verify
Push on the cost of being wrong in either direction

items.prompt: Tell me about a time engineering pushed back on your roadmap. How did you handle it?
followUpHints: Probe for whether they actually changed the plan
Ask what they learned that wasn't in their head before
Push on the long-term relationship: was there fallout?

items.prompt: You're considering a new feature but you're not sure if users actually want it. What's the cheapest, fastest way to find out?
followUpHints: Probe for awareness of qualitative vs. quantitative tools
Ask how many users they'd talk to and how they'd recruit
Push on what would change their mind

items.prompt: Tell me about a launch where you had to cut significant scope close to the ship date. What did you cut, and what did you refuse to?
followUpHints: Probe for the framework or principle behind the call
Ask whether the cuts shipped later, or never
Push on what the team learned about scoping for next time

items.prompt: Tell me about a feature that didn't work, that missed its goal or got rolled back. What happened, and what did you learn?
followUpHints: Probe for honest ownership vs. blaming the team
Ask what they'd do differently in the discovery phase
Push on whether the lesson actually changed their behaviour later

items.prompt: How do you write a spec or PRD? Walk me through your default structure and what you do differently for a one-week project vs. a one-quarter one.
followUpHints: Probe for the level of detail vs. flexibility
Ask how they handle open questions inline
Push on who they share the doc with, and when
